pattern: Building a number from zero with only +1 and global doubling separates into two independent costs: one increment per set bit, plus enough doublings to reach the tallest number's highest bit.
hints: Work out each target value's binary representation: every 1 bit needs its own increment at some point.
Doubling is applied to the whole array at once, so the number of doublings you actually pay for is driven by whichever value needs the most of them, not the sum across all values.
Total calls equal the sum of set bits over every value, plus the largest (bit length minus one) among all values — that single global doubling count covers every element simultaneously.

edge_inputs.description: single zero, already the target, no calls at all
multiple zeros, still nothing to do
a wrong per-element sum of doubling counts overshoots; the real cost only takes the maximum across elements
pure powers of two, one increment each but many shared doublings
all ones, increments only, no doubling ever needed
zeros interleaved with a value needing several increments and shifts

stress.description: large random array spanning the full value range
